name: North Fork Clearwater River
short_description: Public BLM access along the scenic North Fork Clearwater River where rockhounders can find quartz, chalcedony, and agate in river gravels and streamside deposits. No permit required and free to col...
description:
The North Fork Clearwater River in north-central Idaho offers excellent public rockhounding on BLM-managed lands. This region sits within the northern Rocky Mountains where ancient granitic and metamorphic bedrock has weathered and fractured over millions of years, releasing quartz crystals, chalcedony nodules, and colorful agate pieces into the river system. The moderate elevation at 2709 feet and temperate mountain climate make this a productive site for collectors of all levels.

Rockhounds work primarily in the river gravels and along eroded streambanks where seasonal floods sort and expose gemstone materials. The moderate slope terrain is accessible by vehicle, with parking pullouts along roads near the river. Water access is straightforward - the river runs through a mix of forested and open areas, making it pleasant to work during good weather.

This location is ideal for those seeking free, no-permit rockhounding in Idaho's interior. The combination of public access, diverse gem types, and relatively accessible terrain makes it popular with beginner and intermediate collectors. Best finds occur in spring and early summer after snowmelt and flooding have freshly exposed new material in the river gravels.
history: The North Fork Clearwater River drains a region shaped by Precambrian metamorphic rocks and Cretaceous granitic intrusions that form the Idaho Batholith. Quaternary glaciation and ongoing erosion have broken down these feldspathic and quartz-rich bedrock exposures, concentrating weathering-resistant minerals like quartz, chalcedony, and agate in the river's alluvial deposits over thousands of years.
tips: Focus your search in the river gravels and along eroded cutbanks where water action concentrates heavier gem materials - look for areas where the current has slowed and dropped sediment in gentle bends. Bring a classifier or small mesh screen to sift finer gravels quickly; many quality pieces are small to medium-sized and easily missed by eye alone. After spring runoff subsides, the water level drops enough to safely access more of the riverbed - plan visits for June through August when working conditions are best. Use a chisel or small pry bar to loosen tightly cemented gravels in promising zones, and always inspect undercut banks and gravel lenses carefully for concentrated pockets.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2709 feet with moderate slope terrain typical of northern Idaho's river valleys. The site features mixed conifer forest with open streamside areas. Access involves walking from pullouts to the river - distances vary from 50 feet to quarter-mile depending on parking location. Footing is uneven and rocky along the streambank with loose river rocks, mud, and occasional deadfall. During high water (spring-early summer), sections may be impassable or unsafe.
best_season: June - September. Higher elevation and mountain climate mean snowmelt and spring flooding peak April-May, making June onward safer for river access. July-August offer the most stable water levels and driest conditions. September remains productive as nights cool but days stay pleasant.
written_directions: From Orofino, take Highway 12 east approximately 12 miles toward Pierce. Look for pullouts and informal parking areas along or near the North Fork Clearwater River on both sides of the highway. The river runs closely parallel to Highway 12 through this stretch. Smaller dirt and gravel roads branch toward the river in several spots - follow cautiously and park at obvious pullouts. GPS coordinates can be used to locate specific sections, but public access is generally available along BLM lands bordering the river for several miles in this area.
road_conditions: Highway 12 is paved and well-maintained. Access roads to parking pullouts are unpaved, gravel, and minor - suitable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Some pullouts may be muddy in spring or after rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and small parking areas along Highway 12 and side roads near the river. Capacity is typically 2-4 vehicles per pullout. No designated parking lot - expect basic gravel or dirt areas with no facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power equipment. Collect only from public BLM lands - respect private property boundaries. Pack out all trash and minimize site disturbance.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power equipment

Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Orofino, approximately 12 miles west. Full services including gas, restaurants, groceries, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain along the river, but footing is uneven and rocky. No wheelchair access to active collecting areas. Sturdy footwear and good balance required due to loose river rocks and muddy banks. High water in spring may make some areas inaccessible or hazardous. Generally suitable for families with mobile children and adults in reasonable health.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at North Fork Clearwater River in Idaho?
faq.answer: The North Fork Clearwater River is known for quartz crystals, chalcedony pieces, agate, and jasper found in river gravels and streamside deposits. Most material is small to medium-sized and concentrated in the river's alluvial materials rather than in large specimens.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at North Fork Clearwater River?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on this BLM land. Collection is free and open to the public year-round, though you are limited to 25 lbs per day and must collect only from public lands.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound this Idaho river location?
faq.answer: June through September is ideal. While spring snowmelt and flooding expose fresh material, the river is dangerous and water levels block access until June. July and August offer the best combination of stable water levels, dry conditions, and comfortable weather for working the streambed.

faq.question: How far is North Fork Clearwater River from Orofino, and what roads do I need?
faq.answer: The site is about 12 miles east of Orofino via Highway 12. The highway is paved and well-maintained, with informal pullouts and gravel access roads to the river. A passenger vehicle can handle these conditions in good weather.

faq.question: Is North Fork Clearwater River a good rockhounding spot for beginners and kids?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent beginner-friendly location. No permit or experience is required, access is free and easy, and families can collect attractive gem materials with basic tools like a screen or classifier. Kids ages 6+ can participate comfortably with supervision.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring to rockhound at North Fork Clearwater River?
faq.answer: Bring a classifier or mesh screen for sifting river gravels quickly - this is the most efficient method here. A small pry bar or chisel helps loosen cemented gravel zones. Wear sturdy boots for rocky, muddy footing, bring water and sun protection, and consider a small backpack to carry finds up to your 25 lb daily limit.
